title: Atrial Fibrillation
objectives: Recognize ECG findings of atrial fibrillation
Apply rate vs rhythm control strategies
Manage acute rate control in hemodynamically stable patients

vignette.text:
A 72-year-old man presents to the ED with 2 days of palpitations and mild dyspnea on exertion. He noticed his heart 'racing irregularly' yesterday but thought it would pass. He denies chest pain, syncope, or severe dyspnea. He has a history of similar symptoms 6 months ago that resolved spontaneously.

Past Medical History: Hypertension, Type 2 diabetes mellitus, Heart failure with preserved EF (EF 55%), Mild CKD (eGFR 52)

Medications: Lisinopril 20mg daily, Metformin 1000mg BID, Amlodipine 10mg daily

Social History: Retired, drinks 2 glasses of wine nightly, former smoker quit 10 years ago

Allergies: None

physicalExam.general: Alert, comfortable, mild distress from palpitations
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm, no murmurs, JVP mildly elevated

imaging.Echo: EF 55%, left atrial enlargement (4.8 cm), no significant valvular disease

ecg.findings: Irregularly irregular rhythm, absent P waves, fibrillatory baseline, ventricular rate 134 bpm, no ST changes
ecg.interpretation: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

questions.stem: His echo shows left atrial enlargement (4.8 cm).
questions.question: Why is left atrial enlargement associated with atrial fibrillation?

options.text: LA dilation causes stretch-induced remodeling with fibrosis and electrical heterogeneity favoring re-entry

questions.explanation: LA enlargement is both a cause and consequence of AF. Stretch leads to atrial remodeling: fibrosis, ion channel changes, and electrical heterogeneity that create substrate for multiple re-entry wavelets. LA >4.5 cm reduces success of cardioversion and ablation.

questions.stem: AF originates from specific anatomical triggers.
questions.question: What is the most common anatomical trigger site for AF?

options.text: Pulmonary vein ostia - focal triggers that initiate AF

questions.explanation: The pulmonary vein ostia are the most common trigger sites for AF (>90%). Sleeves of atrial myocardium extend into the PVs and can fire rapidly, triggering AF. This is the basis for pulmonary vein isolation ablation procedures.

questions.stem: Stroke prevention is critical in AF.
questions.question: What anatomical structure is the source of most AF-related thrombi?

options.text: Left atrial appendage (LAA) - 90% of AF thrombi form here

questions.explanation: The left atrial appendage is a blind pouch where blood stasis occurs during AF, forming >90% of AF-related thrombi. LAA occlusion devices (Watchman) reduce stroke risk in patients who cannot take anticoagulation.

questions.stem: ECG shows absent P waves and irregularly irregular rhythm.
questions.question: What is the electrophysiologic mechanism of atrial fibrillation?

options.text: Multiple re-entry wavelets in the atria causing chaotic, rapid depolarization (300-600/min)

questions.explanation: AF involves multiple simultaneous re-entry wavelets circulating chaotically through the atria at 300-600 impulses/min. The AV node acts as a filter, allowing only some impulses through, creating the irregular ventricular response. No organized atrial contraction = no P waves.

questions.stem: His heart rate is 134 bpm with irregular rhythm.
questions.question: Why is the ventricular rate irregular in AF?

options.text: AV node receives chaotic atrial impulses and conducts variably based on refractoriness

questions.explanation: The AV node receives 300-600 chaotic atrial impulses/min. Its inherent refractoriness allows only some through, and the variable timing of atrial impulses hitting the AV node creates irregular R-R intervals - the hallmark 'irregularly irregular' rhythm.

questions.stem: He has HFpEF and mild symptoms with AF.
questions.question: Why does AF worsen heart failure symptoms?

options.text: Loss of atrial kick reduces ventricular filling by 15-25%; tachycardia shortens diastole

questions.explanation: AF impairs cardiac output through: (1) Loss of atrial kick (reduces filling by 15-25%, especially important in stiff HFpEF hearts); (2) Tachycardia shortens diastolic filling time; (3) Irregular R-R intervals reduce efficiency. His mild symptoms suggest he's compensating relatively well.

questions.stem: TSH is checked in the workup.
questions.question: Why is thyroid function important to check in new AF?

options.text: Thyroid is not related to AF
Hyperthyroidism increases adrenergic tone and shortens atrial refractoriness, triggering AF

questions.explanation: Hyperthyroidism causes AF in 10-25% of cases through increased beta-adrenergic sensitivity and shortened atrial refractory periods. Subclinical hyperthyroidism also increases AF risk. His normal TSH rules this out as a reversible cause.

questions.stem: He has multiple AF risk factors.
questions.question: What are the major modifiable risk factors for AF?

options.text: Hypertension, obesity, alcohol, sleep apnea, diabetes

questions.explanation: Modifiable risk factors: HTN (increases LA pressure/size), obesity, alcohol (holiday heart), OSA (hypoxia, autonomic changes), diabetes. His HTN and nightly wine are modifiable. Risk factor modification improves rhythm control outcomes.

questions.stem: He had a similar episode 6 months ago that resolved.
questions.question: How is AF classified by pattern?

options.text: Paroxysmal (<7 days, self-terminating), Persistent (>7 days), Long-standing persistent (>1 year), Permanent

questions.explanation: AF classification: Paroxysmal (terminates spontaneously or with intervention within 7 days), Persistent (>7 days), Long-standing persistent (>1 year), Permanent (accepted, no rhythm control attempted). His pattern (recurrent episodes) suggests paroxysmal AF.

questions.stem: AF increases stroke risk.
questions.question: Why does AF increase thromboembolic risk?

options.text: Virchow's triad: stasis (no atrial contraction), endothelial dysfunction, hypercoagulable state
Increased blood flow washes out clots

questions.explanation: AF fulfills Virchow's triad: (1) Stasis in LA/LAA from loss of contraction; (2) Endothelial damage from turbulent flow; (3) Hypercoagulable state (elevated fibrinogen, vWF). AF increases stroke risk 5-fold. Even 'lone AF' has significant risk.

questions.stem: He is hemodynamically stable with HR 134.
questions.question: What is first-line rate control for stable AF with RVR?

options.text: IV beta-blocker (metoprolol) or calcium channel blocker (diltiazem)

questions.explanation: For stable AF-RVR: IV beta-blockers or non-dihydropyridine CCBs (diltiazem, verapamil) are first-line. Both slow AV nodal conduction. Beta-blockers preferred in HF with reduced EF. Diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg IV or metoprolol 5 mg IV. Digoxin slower onset, better as adjunct.

questions.stem: Calculating stroke risk for anticoagulation decision.
questions.question: What is his CHA₂DS₂-VASc score?

options.text: 4 points: Age ≥75 (2) + HTN (1) + DM (1); high stroke risk requiring anticoagulation

questions.explanation: CHA₂DS₂-VASc: CHF (1), HTN (1), Age ≥75 (2), DM (1), Stroke/TIA (2), Vascular disease (1), Age 65-74 (1), Sex female (1). His score: Age 72 ≥75? No, but 65-74 (1), HTN (1), DM (1), HF (1) = 4 points. ≥2 in men = anticoagulation recommended.

questions.stem: He needs anticoagulation with CKD (eGFR 52).
questions.question: What anticoagulation is preferred for AF stroke prevention?

options.text: DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) preferred over warfarin; dose-adjust for renal function

questions.explanation: DOACs are preferred: fewer interactions, no monitoring, lower bleeding risk. For his CrCl ~50: apixaban 5mg BID (standard dose), rivaroxaban 20mg daily, or dabigatran 150mg BID are appropriate. Warfarin reserved for mechanical valves or severe CKD.

questions.stem: Rhythm control may be considered.
questions.question: When is rhythm control preferred over rate control?

options.text: All AF patients need rhythm control
Symptomatic despite rate control, younger patients, first episode, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy

questions.explanation: Rhythm control preferred when: symptomatic despite rate control, younger patients, first episode/paroxysmal AF, tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy, patient preference. EAST-AFNET 4 showed early rhythm control reduces cardiovascular events. Either strategy acceptable if properly anticoagulated.

questions.stem: If rhythm control chosen, antiarrhythmic selection matters.
questions.question: What antiarrhythmics can be used for AF rhythm control in his setting (HFpEF)?

options.text: Flecainide is safe in all patients
Amiodarone or dofetilide (HF-safe); avoid flecainide/propafenone in structural heart disease

questions.explanation: In structural heart disease/HF: amiodarone or dofetilide (sotalol cautious in HF). Class IC drugs (flecainide, propafenone) contraindicated - increase mortality in structural heart disease. His HFpEF limits options. Dofetilide requires in-hospital initiation for QT monitoring.

questions.stem: Deciding on acute management.
questions.question: What determines whether to pursue cardioversion vs rate control acutely?

options.text: Hemodynamic stability, AF duration (<48h or anticoagulated), and symptoms severity

questions.explanation: Decision factors: (1) Unstable (hypotension, ischemia, acute HF) → immediate cardioversion; (2) Stable + AF <48h or adequately anticoagulated → can cardiovert; (3) Stable + AF >48h/unknown + not anticoagulated → rate control, anticoagulate, TEE-guided cardioversion or wait 3 weeks. His AF is >48h, needs anticoagulation first.

questions.stem: His AF has been present for 2 days.
questions.question: Can he be safely cardioverted without prior anticoagulation?

options.text: Controversial - traditionally >48h requires 3 weeks anticoagulation or TEE to exclude thrombus

questions.explanation: Traditional teaching: AF >48h (or unknown duration) requires 3 weeks anticoagulation OR TEE to exclude LA thrombus before cardioversion. His 2-day duration is borderline. Options: TEE now → cardiovert if no thrombus, or rate control + anticoagulate 3 weeks → elective cardioversion.

questions.stem: Planning long-term management.
questions.question: What is essential regardless of rate vs rhythm control strategy?

options.text: Anticoagulation based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score - independent of rhythm
Anticoagulation only needed during AF episodes
Aspirin is sufficient if in sinus rhythm

questions.explanation: Critical point: anticoagulation decision is based on stroke risk factors, NOT rhythm status. Even if successfully cardioverted to sinus rhythm, patients with elevated CHA₂DS₂-VASc need lifelong anticoagulation - AF can recur asymptomatically. His score of 4 requires anticoagulation regardless.

questions.stem: Calculating bleeding risk.
questions.question: What does HAS-BLED score assess?

options.text: Bleeding risk on anticoagulation; high score means modifiable risks, not contraindication

questions.explanation: HAS-BLED: HTN, Abnormal renal/liver, Stroke, Bleeding history, Labile INR, Elderly, Drugs/alcohol. Score ≥3 = high bleeding risk. Importantly, it identifies MODIFIABLE risks (HTN, alcohol, labile INR) rather than contraindication. His wine use is modifiable.

questions.stem: He develops sudden weakness and speech difficulty.
questions.question: What complication must be immediately ruled out?

options.text: Cardioembolic stroke - most common serious AF complication

questions.explanation: Cardioembolic stroke is the most feared AF complication - AF increases stroke risk 5-fold, and AF-related strokes tend to be larger and more disabling. New neurologic symptoms in AF = emergent stroke workup (CT, consider tPA if <4.5h, thrombectomy if LVO).

questions.stem: Uncontrolled AF over months can cause heart failure.
questions.question: What is tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy?

options.text: LV dysfunction from chronically elevated HR that improves with rate/rhythm control

questions.explanation: Persistent tachycardia (even 100-120 bpm) can cause dilated cardiomyopathy over weeks-months. The mechanism involves calcium overload and energetic depletion. Key feature: LV function improves (often normalizes) with rate or rhythm control. Should be considered in new HF with AF.

questions.stem: He is on diltiazem for rate control and develops fatigue.
questions.question: What must be monitored with AV nodal blocking agents in AF?

options.text: Excessive bradycardia and AV block, especially if combined or with underlying conduction disease

questions.explanation: Rate control agents (beta-blockers, CCBs, digoxin) can cause excessive bradycardia, especially in combination or with sick sinus syndrome (tachy-brady syndrome). Monitor for symptomatic bradycardia (fatigue, dizziness, syncope). May need pacemaker if both rate control and rhythm control fail.

questions.stem: Integrating all clinical information.
questions.question: Which BEST synthesizes this presentation?

options.text: Permanent AF requiring only rate control
Paroxysmal AF with RVR in elderly male with high stroke risk (CHA₂DS₂-VASc 4), requiring rate control, anticoagulation, and decision on rhythm strategy

questions.explanation: Synthesis: Recurrent (paroxysmal) AF with RVR, hemodynamically stable, multiple risk factors (HTN, DM, HFpEF, age), high stroke risk requiring anticoagulation, LA enlargement suggesting remodeling. Plan: acute rate control, DOAC initiation, rhythm vs rate strategy discussion.

questions.stem: Planning comprehensive management.
questions.question: What is the comprehensive management plan?

options.text: IV rate control → DOAC initiation → risk factor modification → rate vs rhythm strategy decision → close follow-up

questions.explanation: Comprehensive plan: (1) IV diltiazem/metoprolol for rate control (target HR <110); (2) Start DOAC (apixaban 5mg BID); (3) Address modifiable risks (reduce alcohol, optimize HTN/DM); (4) Discuss rhythm vs rate strategy; (5) If rhythm control: TEE → cardioversion or anticoagulate 3 weeks; (6) Cardiology follow-up.
